results.possibleIdentity: Ellsworth Snyder (Ellsworth J. Snyder, 1931–2005) was an American pianist, conductor, painter, and scholar deeply integrated into the avant-garde art circles of the 1960s and 1970s. He was a close friend and long-time collaborator of the composer John Cage and was associated with the Fluxus movement. Snyder is identified in the 'E.A.T. Datascape,' a digital social history project mapping the participants of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as a significant figure in that network alongside Cage and other artists. He performed Cage's works extensively, including an infamous appearance at the University of Illinois in 1965, and authored a chronological table of Cage's life. He also amassed a significant collection of Fluxus art and was a professor at Milton College and later a resident of Madison, Wisconsin. His work bridged the roles of artist, performer, and historian of the post-WWII avant-garde.

Edward C. Snyder was an engineer associated with Bell Laboratories during the late 1960s and 1970s. Given that Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) was co-founded by Bell Labs engineers (such as Billy Klüver and Fred Waldhauer) and relied on the participation of engineers from the firm to collaborate with artists, it is possible that 'EC Snyder' refers to an engineer participant credited in E.A.T. projects. Historical records from 1977 show him co-authoring technical papers at Bell Labs (e.g., on DC-DC converters). While he is primarily documented as an engineer, the collaborative nature of E.A.T. often led to technical personnel being listed in art catalogs and performance credits from that era.

results.possibleIdentity: Carl Andre (1935–2024) was a prominent American artist and a pioneer of Minimalism, best known for his grid-based and linear floor sculptures. He was highly active during the late 1960s and 1970s, producing works that redefined sculpture by removing it from the pedestal and emphasizing the properties of industrial materials such as bricks, metal plates, and timber. Andre had a direct connection to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement through his participation in the landmark 1966 festival '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' at the 69th Regiment Armory in New York. Specifically, he was involved in Yvonne Rainer's performance piece 'Carriage Discreteness,' which featured a stage environment with materials like cubes, planks, and wood that Andre helped activate. His involvement with the experimental New York art scene of that era also included co-organizing the Art Workers Coalition in 1969. Andre's career included major exhibitions at the Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum (1970) and the Museum of Modern Art (1973), though his legacy remains associated with the controversial death of his third wife, artist Ana Mendieta, in 1985, for which he was acquitted of murder in 1988.

results.possibleIdentity: Carol Alonge is an American contemporary artist and sculptor active since the late 1960s and 1970s, best known for her work with "book objects" and conceptual sculpture. She was a prominent figure in the early artist's book movement, creating works that challenged the traditional physical structure of books. One of her most notable works is "White Sands" (1977), an artist's book consisting of sand encased in vinyl, which is held in the collection of the University of Iowa's Alternative Traditions in the Contemporary Arts. Alonge also created "Sahara, The Longest Desert in the World" (1977), featuring a similar material composition of three pages of sand in vinyl. She was a participant in the seminal touring exhibition "Artists' Books U.S.A." (1978), curated by Peter Frank and Martha Wilson and organized by Independent Curators International (ICI). Her early professional activity includes a 1972 exhibition of sculpture at Pace Editions in New York alongside Roberta Allen. Historically, she is associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), where she served in an administrative capacity as the coordinator for the Technical Services Program, facilitating collaborations between artists and engineers.
